summary: Gruber announces a major change to Daring Fireball's RSS feed structure, making the full content of the site — including Linked List items — freely available to all readers instead of restricting it to paying members. He explains two problems that motivated the change: non-members were confused when Linked List posts didn't appear in their feed, and paying members couldn't use the authenticated feeds with popular readers like Google Reader. He frames the open feed as a month-long experiment for August, funded by weekly exclusive sponsorships, with Rogue Amoeba as the debut sponsor. The move represents a significant shift in Daring Fireball's business model from membership-gated content to sponsorship-supported free distribution.
key_points: The free RSS feed previously only contained article headlines and descriptions, excluding Linked List items, causing reader confusion during periods without full articles
HTTP authentication on member feeds was incompatible with popular aggregators like Google Reader, frustrating the paying members the system was meant to serve
The solution opens the full-content feed to everyone for free, funded by exclusive weekly sponsorships rather than membership fees
The star (★) symbol is introduced to visually distinguish full articles from Linked List entries in the unified feed
The change is positioned as benefiting existing members as much as non-members, since it solves the Google Reader compatibility problem
money_quotes: On most weblogs, everything that appears on the web site also appears in the RSS feed; it's reasonable to assume things are supposed to work that way here, too.
The goal is simple: to make it as easy and convenient as possible for everyone who wants to follow Daring Fireball via a feed subscription.
This experiment with an open-to-all full-content feed is as much for the benefit of members as it is for non-members.
By far, the single most common request for the member feeds is to find a way to make them work with Google Reader. This is it.
For now, let's consider this an experiment for the month of August.
key_insight: When the mechanism meant to serve paying customers actually frustrates them, and confuses everyone else, the right move is to tear it down and find a simpler business model that works with the ecosystem rather than against it.
